quotes.verbatim_quote:
Half-diminished over dominant is a very common substitution and an important one to get under
your fingers.

Playing a half-diminished arpeggio from the 3rd of a dominant chord brings out the 9 sound.
For example: Bm7b5 over G7
quotes.why_load_bearing: This defines the harmonic rationale for the Bm7b5-over-G7 substitution and names the specific interval relationship that makes it work.

quotes.verbatim_quote: Here, I switch from a Bm7b5 arpeggio to a Bdim7 arpeggio, going from the 9 to the b9.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This rule teaches a specific chromatic voice-leading move — substituting the dim7 for the half-dim to introduce the b9 color over a dominant chord.

quotes.verbatim_quote: This pattern can be started on the 5, 1, or #9 of the dominant chord.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This is the key performance rule for Pattern 56, specifying the three valid entry points that keep the chromatic motif functional over a dominant chord.

quotes.verbatim_quote: Pattern 57 starts with a G6 arpeggio and can be used on major or dominant chords.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This defines the dual harmonic function of Pattern 57, allowing the same arpeggio shape to serve two distinct chord qualities.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
The altered scale is the 7th mode of the melodic minor scale.

For example: G altered scale = Ab melodic minor scale starting from the note G.

The altered scale is used to solo over dominant 7th chords, both in major and minor keys.

The altered scale contains all four of the common altered notes (b9-#9-b5-b13), which are used to
create tension over the underlying chord.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This is the book's foundational definition of the altered scale, specifying its modal origin, its harmonic application context, and the four altered tones it supplies.

quotes.verbatim_quote: One of the arpeggios contained in the altered scale is the Gaug7.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This identifies Gaug7 as an extractable arpeggio from the altered scale, giving the player a concrete melodic unit to practice within the scale context.

quotes.verbatim_quote: Another useful arpeggio in the altered scale is the Bmaj7#5.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This names a second extractable arpeggio from the G altered scale, expanding the player's vocabulary of melodic options over a G7alt chord.

quotes.verbatim_quote: Another useful arpeggio in the altered scale is the Abm/maj7.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This names a third distinct arpeggio available within the G altered scale, completing a set of three superimposition targets the book systematically presents.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
The bebop scale is an 8-note scale.

It is constructed by adding a chromatic passing note to 7-note scales such as the Mixolydian,
Dorian, and Ionan modes.

There are three kinds of bebop scales

1. The dominant bebop scale — Mixolydian + 7
2. The major bebop scale — Major scale + b6
3. The minor bebop scale — Dorian + 7
quotes.why_load_bearing: This is the book's definitive taxonomy of the bebop scale family, specifying the construction rule and naming all three variants with their added chromatic tone.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
The bebop scale gives you a framework for adding chromatic notes to your jazz solos.

e The bebop scale adds a sense of tension and release to your soloing ideas.

e When you start the bebop scale on a downbeat and on a chord tone, all the following chord tones
of the scale will fall on downbeats and the other notes of the scale on upbeats.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This passage states the three core practical benefits of the bebop scale, with the third being the metric alignment rule that is the scale's defining pedagogical payoff.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
This will always happen, as long as you start the bebop scale on a downbeat and on a chord note (1,
3, 5, or b7).
quotes.why_load_bearing: This formulates the invariant rule governing the bebop scale's metric property — the specific entry conditions (downbeat + chord tone) that guarantee chord tones land on beats.
